fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that HTTP client toolkit we've been talking about. The goal is basically to let our app devs stop writing all that repetitive glue code every time they spin up a new service — you know, the stuff where everyone hand-rolls their own URL building, param encoding, header juggling, etc. It always ends up slightly broken in some edge case.

The toolkit should handle building requests declaratively, deal with the auth token situation automatically when the server pushes back (similar to how we handled the refresh flow in the old login module, check that for reference), and support attaching middleware so teams can inject stuff like tracing headers without touching the core logic.

We also need decent logging — both for outbound requests and what comes back — and a way to render requests as copy-pasteable shell commands for debugging. On the data side, it needs to handle nested structures and collections in query params, not just flat key=value stuff. There's also a file upload scenario we need to support, but only byte-array payloads — if someone tries to pass something weird there it should fail cleanly without leaking internal error details.

The whole thing should be organized properly across multiple files, not one giant blob. Output from the adapter layer should always be deterministic and machine-readable. Let's get a proper breakdown of what needs to be built.

One extra pass on the behavior details since a few folks asked. For query strings, by default if a key’s value is null we just leave it out completely, so that key produces nothing. If includeNullQueryVars is set to true, then null comes through as a bare key= with an empty value. Empty string values always emit key= no matter what. For lists, each item becomes its own repeated key=value pair with the same key, like foo=bar&foo=baz. Empty-string and null elements inside a list are skipped entirely, but whitespace-only elements are kept and percent-encoded. And if useBrackets is enabled, list keys get the percent-encoded [] suffix, so you’d see foo%5B%5D=bar.

For nested data, maps flatten with dot notation as deep as needed, like parent.child=value and grandparent.parent.child=value. If useBrackets is enabled, those nested map keys switch to bracket form instead, so parent%5Bchild%5D=value. If there’s a nested list under one of those paths, it keeps repeating that flattened key. Also on scalar rendering, numbers and booleans should just come out in their textual form. Trailing zeros in a fractional number are dropped, so 123.450 becomes 123.45, and booleans are always true or false. Same rule for top-level scalar values and list elements.

On deterministic output, all map-like outputs like headers and fields need to be sorted by key. Structured decoded values should be rendered as canonical JSON, while plain strings stay verbatim. Every output line is key=value, one entry per line. For multipart specifically, the output should list the resulting fields first, sorted by name, in the form field.<name>=<value>, then file_count=<n>, then each file part as file.<name>.bytes=<comma-separated byte values>. Fields are sorted, but file parts should stay in the order they were provided.

For debugging output, the curl format should be exactly curl -v -X <METHOD> followed by each header as -H '<name>: <value>', then -d '<body>' if there is a body, and then the quoted URL as "<url>". If the body encoder adds headers like content-type, those should show up there too. The request log format starts with --> <METHOD> <URL>, then one line per header as <name>: <value>, then the body, then --> END <METHOD> (<n>-byte body).

On responses, any 2xx status is treated as success and should emit response_status=<n>, response_successful=true, and response_body=<body>. Anything outside 2xx is unsuccessful and should emit response_status=<n>, response_successful=false, and response_error=<error_payload> instead of response_body.

And for the auth retry piece, when a 401 comes back, the Authenticator interceptor should attach an authorization header with the provided token and replay the request once, using a pluggable authenticator abstraction in the client dispatch layer. Also worth being really strict on error output: all errors should come out as a single neutral line in the form error=<category>, and the only categories we should emit are base_uri_has_query or unsupported_part_type. No exception class names, no stack traces, and no runtime-generated message text in stdout output.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main() async { final input = jsonDecode(await stdin.transform(utf8.decoder).join()); final op = input['op'] as String; ... }

trigger_keywords: how does path trimming work
slash deduplication in URL joining
trim delimiter from path segment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The trim operation takes a text, a mode (left, right, or both), and an optional single delimiter character. For each affected side, whitespace is removed first; then if a delimiter character was supplied and exactly one occurrence sits at that edge, that single occurrence is also removed. Only one delimiter character is removed per side — so '//foo' left-trimmed by '/' yields '/foo', not 'foo'.

trigger_keywords: base URL with query parameters
what if the base URI already has query params
error when base URL contains query string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A base URI that carries query parameters is a configuration error. The system must reject it and emit the neutral error category 'error=base_uri_has_query' with no language runtime details or exception messages leaked. Default query parameters must be supplied through a parameter map or interceptor, never baked into the base URI.

trigger_keywords: how is the URL composed from base and path
what happens when path is an absolute URL
merging base URL and request path
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly one slash separates base and path regardless of trailing/leading slashes on either side. If the path is itself an absolute http/https URL, the base is ignored entirely. Query parameters already present on the base are dropped. Query parameters on the path are preserved and merged with the explicit parameter map (path query first, then map parameters).

trigger_keywords: how do headers get merged or overridden
override existing header behavior
header apply override flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: By default, applying a header overwrites any existing header with the same name. When the override flag is set to false, the existing value is kept and the incoming header is ignored. Output always lists the resulting headers sorted by name.

trigger_keywords: how does auth retry work
automatic token refresh on 401
re-attach token and replay request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a request returns a 401 Unauthorized status, the authenticator attaches an authorization token (as the 'authorization' header) and replays the request exactly once. The output reports: first_response_status=401, retried_authorization=<token_value>, final_response_status=<status>, final_response_body=<body>. This mirrors the auth refresh pattern in the existing codebase's interceptor chain.

trigger_keywords: what body converters are supported
how is the request body serialized
JSON vs form encoding for request body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two converters are supported. The JSON converter serializes the body as JSON and sets content-type to 'application/json; charset=utf-8'. The form converter encodes the map as key=value pairs and sets content-type to 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'. A plain string body sets content-type to 'text/plain; charset=utf-8'. The content-type header only appears in request output when a body is present.

trigger_keywords: multipart file upload supported types
what is a valid file part value
unsupported part type error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A file part must carry a byte list (array of integers) as its value. Supplying a bare scalar such as a number is invalid and must be reported as 'error=unsupported_part_type' with no language runtime detail or exception message leaked. Parts whose value is null are skipped entirely. Value parts become form fields whose value is the stringified input.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'multipart', 'serialize_query', 'trim_text', 'resolve_url', 'assemble_request', 'set_headers', 'http_request', 'decode_body', 'auth_retry', 'curl', 'log_request', 'log_response'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: organized properly across multiple files, not one giant blob
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The architecture requires separate modules for: query encoding, URL composition/trimming, request modeling, header utilities, body converters, client dispatch, authenticator/retry logic, interceptors/logging, multipart assembly, and a thin execution adapter for stdin/stdout JSON command routing

context_pointers.vague_reference: output from the adapter layer should always be deterministic and machine-readable
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The stdout contract is line-oriented key=value format; map-like outputs (headers, multipart fields) must be sorted by key; the execution adapter is the only component that knows about the JSON command envelope (op field) and stdin/stdout — core library components must not reference these

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific guidance on from the business side. You'll want to look at the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the exact behavioral contract — I can speak to what the feature should accomplish from a user perspective but the precise encoding or formatting rules are defined there.

feature10_1_multipart_build.json.description: Build a multipart/form-data request from a list of parts. A value part becomes a form field whose value is the stringified input. A file part carrying a byte list becomes a file upload. Parts whose value is null are skipped entirely. Output lists the resulting fields (sorted by name), the number of file parts, and the raw bytes of each file part.

feature10_2_multipart_error.json.description: Reject an unsupported file part payload. A file part must carry a byte list (or an already-built file object); supplying a bare scalar such as a number is invalid and reported as a neutral error category, with no language runtime detail leaked.

feature1_1_query_scalars.json.description: Serialize a flat key/value map into a URL query string. Each entry becomes key=value with the value percent-encoded. By default keys whose value is null are omitted entirely; enabling include-null emits them as bare key= entries. Empty-string values always emit key= ; whitespace is percent-encoded (space -> %20, tab -> %09); booleans and numbers render as their textual form.

feature1_2_query_lists.json.description: Serialize a map whose value is a list. Each list element is emitted as a separate key=value pair that repeats the same key. Empty-string and null elements inside the list are skipped; whitespace-only elements are kept and percent-encoded.

feature1_3_query_brackets.json.description: Serialize collection values using bracket notation. When bracket mode is enabled, list keys gain a percent-encoded [] suffix (%5B%5D) and nested map keys are wrapped as key%5Bsubkey%5D instead of the dotted form.

feature1_4_query_nested_maps.json.description: Serialize a map that contains nested maps. Nested keys are flattened using a dot separator (parent.child=value) to arbitrary depth. A nested list keeps repeating its flattened key. Null leaf values are omitted unless include-null is enabled, in which case they emit the flattened key with an empty value.

feature2_string_trim.json.description: Trim a string. Mode 'left' removes leading whitespace; 'right' removes trailing whitespace; 'both' removes both. When an optional single delimiter character is supplied, after the whitespace on that side is removed, exactly one occurrence of that character (if present at the edge) is also removed.

feature3_url_composition.json.description: Join a base URL and a request path into one absolute URL, then append query parameters. Exactly one slash separates the base and path regardless of trailing/leading slashes on either side. If the path is itself an absolute http/https URL, the base is ignored. Query parameters already present on the base are dropped; query parameters present on the path are kept and merged with the explicit parameter map (path-first, then map).

feature4_1_request_build.json.description: Construct an HTTP request from a method, a relative or absolute request URI, a base URI, an optional parameter map and headers. The output reports the resolved method, the fully composed URL (base+path+merged query) and every header sorted by name. Query parameters embedded in the URI are preserved and merged with the explicit parameter map; nested maps flatten with a dot separator and lists repeat their key.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
url=https://foo/bar
header.accept=application/json; charset=utf-8
header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8

feature4_2_request_baseuri_query.json.description: Reject a base URI that itself carries query parameters. Constructing a request with such a base URI is a configuration error: default query parameters must be supplied through a parameter map or interceptor, never baked into the base. The error is reported as a neutral category with no language runtime detail.

feature5_header_apply.json.description: Apply one header (name+value) or a batch of headers onto an existing request's header set, producing a new header set. By default an existing header with the same name is overwritten; when override is disabled, the existing value is kept and the incoming one ignored. Output lists the resulting headers sorted by name.

feature6_1_http_methods.json.description: Issue an HTTP call through the client for a given method, path and query, optionally encoding a structured body with the JSON or form converter and applying a header-injecting interceptor. Output echoes the outgoing request as actually sent to the transport: method, fully-qualified URL, headers (sorted, including any content-type the converter set), and the serialized body. The JSON converter sets content-type application/json and JSON-encodes the body; the form converter sets content-type application/x-www-form-urlencoded and encodes the map as key=value pairs.

cases.expected_output:
request_method=GET
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/get?key=val
request_header.foo=bar
request_header.int=42
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=get response

request_method=POST
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/post?key=val
request_header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
request_header.foo=bar
request_header.int=42
request_body={"content":"body"}
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=post response

request_method=PUT
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/put?key=val
request_header.content-type=application/json; charset=utf-8
request_header.foo=bar
request_header.int=42
request_body={"content":"body"}
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=put response

request_method=DELETE
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/delete?key=val
request_header.foo=bar
request_header.int=42
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=delete response

request_method=POST
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/map
request_header.content-type=application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=utf-8
request_body=foo=test&default=hello
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=ok

feature6_2_response_status.json.description: Surface the outcome of an HTTP call. A 2xx status yields a successful response carrying the decoded body; a non-2xx status yields an unsuccessful response carrying the error payload instead of a body. Output reports the status code, a success flag, and either the body (on success) or the error (on failure).

cases.expected_output:
request_method=GET
request_url=http://localhost:8000/test/get
request_header.int=42
response_status=200
response_successful=true
response_body=get response

feature7_json_decode.json.description: Decode a JSON response payload into the requested shape. A quoted JSON string decodes to its inner text; a JSON array decodes to a list of elements; a JSON object decodes to a key/value map. The decoded value is rendered back as canonical JSON (or the raw text for a plain string).

feature8_auth_retry.json.description: Automatically retry a request once after an authentication challenge. The first attempt returns 401 Unauthorized; an authenticator then attaches an authorization token and the request is replayed. Output reports the first attempt's status, the authorization token observed on the retried request, and the final status and body after the successful retry.

feature9_1_curl.json.description: Render an outgoing request as an equivalent curl command line, including the method (-X), each header (-H), the body (-d) and the quoted target URL. Headers added by the body encoder (such as content-type for a text body) appear in the command.

feature9_2_log_request.json.description: Emit a human-readable multi-line log of an outgoing request: a start line with method and URL, one line per header, the body, and an end line stating the method and the body size in bytes.

cases.expected_output:
--> POST base/
foo: bar
content-type: text/plain; charset=utf-8
test
--> END POST (4-byte body)

feature9_3_log_response.json.description: Emit a human-readable multi-line log of a received response: a status line with status code and URL, one line per response header, the response body, and an end line stating the originating method and the body size in bytes.

cases.expected_output:
<-- 200 base/
foo: bar
responseBodyBase
--> END POST (16-byte body)
